old_claim.note: Technical Drawing Standards provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: The 1570 atlas does not depend on the separately scoped 1588 machine-book drawing standards; uniform atlas engraving is already represented in its cartography edge.

source_shape.source_locator: Museum Plantin-Moretus dates the first Ortelius atlas edition to 1570 and describes maps engraved in a uniform format for one volume.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The museum attributes the atlas's uniform format to Ortelius's map selection and engraving process at the time of the 1570 edition.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Uniform layout within an atlas is not the same scoped technology as late Renaissance machine-book drawing standards, which the graph dates eighteen years later.

ontology_before: The first printed atlas depended on a later technical-drawing node scoped to machine-book conventions.
ontology_after: The atlas depends on cartography and printing while its internal uniformity remains part of the atlas claim itself.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: printed_map_atlases no longer directly depends on technical_drawing_standards.
would_reject_if: The technical_drawing_standards node were resourced and redated to a pre-1570 convention directly used to produce Ortelius's atlas.
short_reason: The former prerequisite postdates the atlas and represents a different drawing practice.
